prevalence.trend: stable; improved recognition in urban centers

burden.mortalityRate: Low; risk via AF and untreated thyroid storm (rare)

vulnerablePopulations: patients with limited access to follow-up testing
epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: Low TSH with elevated free T4/T3; TRAb and imaging for etiology where available; diagnosis often clinical + lab in primary care/tertiary centers

publicHealthSystem.screeningCoverage: no routine screening; testing is symptom-driven
publicHealthSystem.controlRate: Variable due to follow-up gaps and cost of repeated testing

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: TSH/T4 testing more available in urban areas; imaging and endocrinology services concentrated in tertiary centers
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: Methimazole/carbimazole; beta-blockers; radioiodine or surgery in select cases; pregnancy-specific regimens (PTU in early pregnancy) in specialist care
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: Access to endocrinology, repeated lab monitoring costs, and delayed definitive therapy outside metros
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: Affordable generic antithyroid drugs and expanding diagnostic capacity in larger cities
